texts.label: text
texts.orig: Trees stand in the Sun and give shade to others. Their fruits are also for others. Likewise, good people bear all hardships and bring welfare to others. They give to others whatever they have earned.
texts.text: Trees stand in the Sun and give shade to others. Their fruits are also for others. Likewise, good people bear all hardships and bring welfare to others. They give to others whatever they have earned.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Wise saying)
texts.text: (Wise saying)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is a pleasant morning after yesterday's refreshing rain. Dr Raghu and Maniram chacha (uncle) have been invited to the school by the science teacher, Madam Sulekha, to
texts.text: It is a pleasant morning after yesterday's refreshing rain. Dr Raghu and Maniram chacha (uncle) have been invited to the school by the science teacher, Madam Sulekha, to

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prepare them for the nature walk, Dr Raghu informs the students that the objective of this walk is to experience the beauty and variety of plants and animals in the nature. The students are excited to join them. They are curious to interact and learn from them. The teacher advises the students to carry a notebook, a pen and a water bottle.
texts.text: To prepare them for the nature walk, Dr Raghu informs the students that the objective of this walk is to experience the beauty and variety of plants and animals in the nature. The students are excited to join them. They are curious to interact and learn from them. The teacher advises the students to carry a notebook, a pen and a water bottle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As they walk, they begin exploring the plants and animals around them. Dr Raghu advises the students to notice the variety of smells in the park and emphasises respecting all living creatures and observing them without disturbing. Maniram chacha tells the students to not only observe different plants and animals but also to carefully listen to different sounds. The students come across a variety of plants, including grasses, bushes, and large trees. They also observe a variety of birds sitting on the branches of trees, butterflies moving from flower to flower and monkeys jumping from one tree to another. They record their observations in their notebooks and discuss them with Dr Raghu and Maniram chacha.
texts.text: As they walk, they begin exploring the plants and animals around them. Dr Raghu advises the students to notice the variety of smells in the park and emphasises respecting all living creatures and observing them without disturbing. Maniram chacha tells the students to not only observe different plants and animals but also to carefully listen to different sounds. The students come across a variety of plants, including grasses, bushes, and large trees. They also observe a variety of birds sitting on the branches of trees, butterflies moving from flower to flower and monkeys jumping from one tree to another. They record their observations in their notebooks and discuss them with Dr Raghu and Maniram chacha.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The students can hear the chirping of birds. Dr Raghu informs them that each bird has a unique chirp. This is an example of diversity in nature. Dr Raghu requests Maniram ,,,chacha to mimic calls of some birds. Maniram chacha 'mimics different bird calls. The students enthusiastically start copying him.
texts.text: The students can hear the chirping of birds. Dr Raghu informs them that each bird has a unique chirp. This is an example of diversity in nature. Dr Raghu requests Maniram chacha to mimic calls of some birds. Maniram chacha mimics different bird calls. The students enthusiastically start copying him.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you ever observed different plants and animals around you? Share and discuss your observations with your friends and teacher.
texts.text: Have you ever observed different plants and animals around you? Share and discuss your observations with your friends and teacher.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Diversity in Plants and Animals Around Us
texts.text: 2.1 Diversity in Plants and Animals Around Us

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.1: Let us explore and record
texts.text: Activity 2.1: Let us explore and record

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Plan a nature walk with your teacher to a park or a nearby forest.
texts.text: ¢ Plan a nature walk with your teacher to a park or a nearby forest.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ While on the nature walk, observe different plants, insects, birds, and other animals. Also, note the weather conditions, whether it is hot, cold, windy and so on.
texts.text: @ While on the nature walk, observe different plants, insects, birds, and other animals. Also, note the weather conditions, whether it is hot, cold, windy and so on.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ You can collect different types of fallen leaves or flowers and create a scrapbook.
texts.text: ¢ You can collect different types of fallen leaves or flowers and create a scrapbook.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Take care of the plants and animals in nature. Ensure that you do not disturb the plants and animals in the park. Do not pluck leaves and flowers.
texts.text: @ Take care of the plants and animals in nature. Ensure that you do not disturb the plants and animals in the park. Do not pluck leaves and flowers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Record your observations in Table 2.1 about the features of stems, leaves, flowers and anything interesting in various plants. Some examples have been given for you in Fig. 2.1 and Table 2.1.
texts.text: ¢ Record your observations in Table 2.1 about the features of stems, leaves, flowers and anything interesting in various plants. Some examples have been given for you in Fig. 2.1 and Table 2.1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What similarities and differences did you find among the plants that you observed?
texts.text: What similarities and differences did you find among the plants that you observed?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You must have observed that plants have a variety of features such as—
texts.text: You must have observed that plants have a variety of features such as—

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ tall/short, hard/soft stem
texts.text: ¢ tall/short, hard/soft stem

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, create a list of animals you observed during this walk or from your previous experiences. Record the places where they live, the food they eat and the way they move around in Table 2.2. Some examples have been provided for you.
texts.text: Now, create a list of animals you observed during this walk or from your previous experiences. Record the places where they live, the food they eat and the way they move around in Table 2.2. Some examples have been provided for you.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the similarities and differences among the animals that you have observed and recorded in Table 2.2?
texts.text: What are the similarities and differences among the animals that you have observed and recorded in Table 2.2?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You would have observed that some animals live on land while some others live on trees. Birds live on trees. Fish live in water and some animals like frogs live on land as well as in water. Animals consume a diverse range of foods and exhibit a variety of movements.
texts.text: You would have observed that some animals live on land while some others live on trees. Birds live on trees. Fish live in water and some animals like frogs live on land as well as in water. Animals consume a diverse range of foods and exhibit a variety of movements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sketch the plants and animals observed by you in your notebook or prepare a scrapbook with leaves, flowers from different plants and feathers from animals. Write all the details you have gathered about them.
texts.text: Sketch the plants and animals observed by you in your notebook or prepare a scrapbook with leaves, flowers from different plants and feathers from animals. Write all the details you have gathered about them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While travelling to and from school, observe your surroundings and look out for a variety of plants and animals. Add the name of any plant or animal that you have not listed before in Tables 2.1 and 2.2.
texts.text: While travelling to and from school, observe your surroundings and look out for a variety of plants and animals. Add the name of any plant or animal that you have not listed before in Tables 2.1 and 2.2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.2: Let us appreciate
texts.text: Activity 2.2: Let us appreciate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Close your eyes for 30 seconds and think of one plant and one animal that you have closely observed and appreciated very much.
texts.text: @ Close your eyes for 30 seconds and think of one plant and one animal that you have closely observed and appreciated very much.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Now each one of you can draw the plant and animal that you thought of on the blackboard.
texts.text: @ Now each one of you can draw the plant and animal that you thought of on the blackboard.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ What are your observations about the various plants and animals that have been drawn?
texts.text: @ What are your observations about the various plants and animals that have been drawn?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ How many different plants and animals did the entire class draw on the blackboard?
texts.text: ¢ How many different plants and animals did the entire class draw on the blackboard?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Do you think that there may be many more varieties of plants and animals other than those drawn on the board?
texts.text: ¢ Do you think that there may be many more varieties of plants and animals other than those drawn on the board?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The variety of plants and animals found in a particular region contributes to the biodiversity of that region.
texts.text: The variety of plants and animals found in a particular region contributes to the biodiversity of that region.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Each member in the biodiversity of a region has a different role to play. For example, trees provide food and shelter to some birds and other animals, animals help in spreading seeds after eating fruits, and so on. Can you think of more such examples? This shows that plants and animals are dependent on each other.
texts.text: Each member in the biodiversity of a region has a different role to play. For example, trees provide food and shelter to some birds and other animals, animals help in spreading seeds after eating fruits, and so on. Can you think of more such examples? This shows that plants and animals are dependent on each other.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 How to Group Plants and Animals?
texts.text: 2.2 How to Group Plants and Animals?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How would you arrange your books and notebooks in groups? Would arranging them in groups help you better organise your school bag?
texts.text: How would you arrange your books and notebooks in groups? Would arranging them in groups help you better organise your school bag?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, let us look at the world around us. We are surrounded by a variety of plants and animals with different features about which you have learnt in section 2.1. We can group them based on similarities and differences among them.
texts.text: Now, let us look at the world around us. We are surrounded by a variety of plants and animals with different features about which you have learnt in section 2.1. We can group them based on similarities and differences among them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.3: Let US QrOUP)
texts.text: Activity 2.3: Let US QrOUP)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Collect pictures of various other plants and animals. Cut their pictures from old magazines, newspapers, charts and other sources. Paste each of these pictures on a different card.
texts.text: ¢ Collect pictures of various other plants and animals. Cut their pictures from old magazines, newspapers, charts and other sources. Paste each of these pictures on a different card.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Divide your class in groups of 5-6 students each.
texts.text: ¢ Divide your class in groups of 5-6 students each.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a4 Pool the cards prepared by the students in your group.
texts.text: a4 Pool the cards prepared by the students in your group.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Observe various features of plants and animals shown on the cards.
texts.text: ¢ Observe various features of plants and animals shown on the cards.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Recall the features of plants and animals that you have listed in Tables 2.1 and 2.2.
texts.text: ¢ Recall the features of plants and animals that you have listed in Tables 2.1 and 2.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Group them on the basis of common features.
texts.text: ¢ Group them on the basis of common features.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Share and discuss the basis of grouping you have made with other groups in your class.
texts.text: ¢ Share and discuss the basis of grouping you have made with other groups in your class.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You will be surprised to see that the basis used by different groups may vary. What do you think are the reasons behind it? Different students might have chosen different common features for the grouping. For example, some students may have chosen the height of plants as the basis for grouping while others might have chosen presence or absence of flowers as the basis for grouping of plants (See Fig. 2.2).
texts.text: You will be surprised to see that the basis used by different groups may vary. What do you think are the reasons behind it? Different students might have chosen different common features for the grouping. For example, some students may have chosen the height of plants as the basis for grouping while others might have chosen presence or absence of flowers as the basis for grouping of plants (See Fig. 2.2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Presence/ absence of flowers
texts.text: Presence/ absence of flowers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hard/soft stem
texts.text: Hard/soft stem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Eating habits
texts.text: Eating habits

texts.text: Place they live

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.2: Some possible criteria of groupings of plants and animals
texts.text: Fig. 2.2: Some possible criteria of groupings of plants and animals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have grouped animals based on varied features, such as what they eat, where they live, what colour they are and how they move.
texts.text: You may have grouped animals based on varied features, such as what they eat, where they live, what colour they are and how they move.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What is the importance of grouping? Grouping makes it easier to understand and study plants and animals on the basis of their similarities and differences.
texts.text: What is the importance of grouping? Grouping makes it easier to understand and study plants and animals on the basis of their similarities and differences.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You will learn more about the importance of grouping in our daily lives in the chapter, 'Materials Around Us'.
texts.text: You will learn more about the importance of grouping in our daily lives in the chapter, 'Materials Around Us'.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.1 How to group plants?
texts.text: 2.2.1 How to group plants?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You must have noticed that plants show variation in the features related to stems, leaves, flowers, and more. The stems of different plants vary in thickness, height, and hardness, while the leaves vary in shape, colour, size and arrangement. You might have tried grouping the plants in Activity 2.3 using one of these features.
texts.text: You must have noticed that plants show variation in the features related to stems, leaves, flowers, and more. The stems of different plants vary in thickness, height, and hardness, while the leaves vary in shape, colour, size and arrangement. You might have tried grouping the plants in Activity 2.3 using one of these features.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might have also learnt in earlier classes that plants can be grouped into herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their height and types of stem. Let us study the features of plants in more detail and group them on that basis.
texts.text: You might have also learnt in earlier classes that plants can be grouped into herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their height and types of stem. Let us study the features of plants in more detail and group them on that basis.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.4: Let us group
texts.text: Activity 2.4: Let us group

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Let us go on a nature walk again for some more interesting observations.
texts.text: Let us go on a nature walk again for some more interesting observations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Look closely at the height of different plants. Are these plants shorter than you, as tall as you, or taller than you?
texts.text: @ Look closely at the height of different plants. Are these plants shorter than you, as tall as you, or taller than you?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Is the stem brown or green? Touch and feel the stems and try to bend them gently. Can you bend the stem easily, or is it stiff? Take care that the stems do not break.
texts.text: @ Is the stem brown or green? Touch and feel the stems and try to bend them gently. Can you bend the stem easily, or is it stiff? Take care that the stems do not break.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Also, observe from where the branches of the plants arise—whether they arise close to the ground or higher up on the stem. Fill in your observations in Table 2.3. A few examples are already given.
texts.text: ¢ Also, observe from where the branches of the plants arise—whether they arise close to the ground or higher up on the stem. Fill in your observations in Table 2.3. A few examples are already given.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What differences do you observe among herbs, shrubs, and trees? How can you group plants as herbs, shrubs, and trees based on the data entered in Table 2.3?
texts.text: What differences do you observe among herbs, shrubs, and trees? How can you group plants as herbs, shrubs, and trees based on the data entered in Table 2.3?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some plants grow really tall and have hard, thick, brown, and woody stems. Their branches typically start higher up on the stem and away from the ground. These plants are called trees. For example, a mango tree (Fig. 2.3a).
texts.text: Some plants grow really tall and have hard, thick, brown, and woody stems. Their branches typically start higher up on the stem and away from the ground. These plants are called trees. For example, a mango tree (Fig. 2.3a).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (b) Shrub
texts.text: (b) Shrub

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (c) Herb
texts.text: (c) Herb

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.3: Types of plants
texts.text: Fig. 2.3: Types of plants

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some plants are not as tall as trees. These plants often have many brown woody stems that start branching very close to the ground. These stems are hard but not as thick as the stem of a tree. These plants are called shrubs. For example, a rose plant is a shrub (Fig. 2.3b).
texts.text: Some plants are not as tall as trees. These plants often have many brown woody stems that start branching very close to the ground. These stems are hard but not as thick as the stem of a tree. These plants are called shrubs. For example, a rose plant is a shrub (Fig. 2.3b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some plants are typically small with soft and green stems. These are known as herbs. For example, a tomato plant is a herb (Fig. 2.3c).
texts.text: Some plants are typically small with soft and green stems. These are known as herbs. For example, a tomato plant is a herb (Fig. 2.3c).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some plants with weak stems need support to climb and grow, and are called climbers. Some plants creep along the ground and are called creepers.
texts.text: Some plants with weak stems need support to climb and grow, and are called climbers. Some plants creep along the ground and are called creepers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What can be other features on the basis of which you can group plants? Let us perform another activity.
texts.text: What can be other features on the basis of which you can group plants? Let us perform another activity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.5: Let us compare
texts.text: Activity 2.5: Let us compare

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Look at the leaves of different plants collected by you, during the nature walk.
texts.text: ¢ Look at the leaves of different plants collected by you, during the nature walk.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Do you notice any variation in the shape and structure of these leaves?
texts.text: @ Do you notice any variation in the shape and structure of these leaves?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may observe thin lines on the leaves of the plants (Fig. 2.4a). These are veins. The pattern of veins on the leaf is called venation. What differences do you see in the veins of leaves shown in Fig. 2.4a and Fig. 2.4b?
texts.text: You may observe thin lines on the leaves of the plants (Fig. 2.4a). These are veins. The pattern of veins on the leaf is called venation. What differences do you see in the veins of leaves shown in Fig. 2.4a and Fig. 2.4b?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.4: Leaves showing different types of venation
texts.text: Fig. 2.4: Leaves showing different types of venation

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (a) Hibiscus leaf with reticulate venation
texts.text: (a) Hibiscus leaf with reticulate venation

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) Banana leaf with parallel venation
texts.text: (b) Banana leaf with parallel venation

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (c) Grass leaf with parallel venation
texts.text: (c) Grass leaf with parallel venation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In some leaves, you can observe a net-like pattern of veins on both sides of a thick middle vein. This pattern is called reticulate venation. For example, leaves of hibiscus exhibit reticulate venation (Fig. 2.4a). In some leaves, you may observe that the veins run parallel. This pattern is called parallel venation. For example, the leaves of banana plants and grasses exhibit parallel venation (Fig. 2.4b and Fig. 2.4c).
texts.text: In some leaves, you can observe a net-like pattern of veins on both sides of a thick middle vein. This pattern is called reticulate venation. For example, leaves of hibiscus exhibit reticulate venation (Fig. 2.4a). In some leaves, you may observe that the veins run parallel. This pattern is called parallel venation. For example, the leaves of banana plants and grasses exhibit parallel venation (Fig. 2.4b and Fig. 2.4c).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you think that plants can be grouped on the basis of venation present in their leaves?
texts.text: Do you think that plants can be grouped on the basis of venation present in their leaves?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, let us try to explore roots of the plants. Do all plants have roots? Are these roots similar?
texts.text: Now, let us try to explore roots of the plants. Do all plants have roots? Are these roots similar?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.6: Let us find OUt
texts.text: Activity 2.6: Let us find OUt

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Visit an open area where wild herbs and grasses are growing. You may use small herbs for this exercise.
texts.text: ¢ Visit an open area where wild herbs and grasses are growing. You may use small herbs for this exercise.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Using a khurpi (trowel), carefully dig out a few different herbs without damaging the roots. To do this, you may wet the soil and loosen it.
texts.text: ¢ Using a khurpi (trowel), carefully dig out a few different herbs without damaging the roots. To do this, you may wet the soil and loosen it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Wash the roots with water and observe them.
texts.text: ¢ Wash the roots with water and observe them.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ After you are done observing, make sure to replant the herbs so that they may continue to thrive and grow.
texts.text: ¢ After you are done observing, make sure to replant the herbs so that they may continue to thrive and grow.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the similarities and differences in the roots of the plants collected by you? What differences do you see in the roots of plants shown in Fig. 2.5a and Fig. 2.5b?
texts.text: What are the similarities and differences in the roots of the plants collected by you? What differences do you see in the roots of plants shown in Fig. 2.5a and Fig. 2.5b?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (a) Taproot system in a mustard plant
texts.text: (a) Taproot system in a mustard plant

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (b) Fibrous root system in common grass
texts.text: (b) Fibrous root system in common grass

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.5: Types of roots
texts.text: Fig. 2.5: Types of roots

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carefully observe the roots of a mustard plant in Fig. 2.5a. The roots of this plant consist of one main root and small side roots arising from it. The main root is called taproot. Another example of a plant having taproots is hibiscus observed by you in Activity 2.1. The plant in Fig. 2.5b is a common grass plant. The roots of this plant appear as a bunch of similar-sized thin roots arising from the base of the stem. Such roots are called fibrous roots (Fig. 2.5b). Does your collection include any other grasses? What kind of roots do they have?
texts.text: Carefully observe the roots of a mustard plant in Fig. 2.5a. The roots of this plant consist of one main root and small side roots arising from it. The main root is called taproot. Another example of a plant having taproots is hibiscus observed by you in Activity 2.1. The plant in Fig. 2.5b is a common grass plant. The roots of this plant appear as a bunch of similar-sized thin roots arising from the base of the stem. Such roots are called fibrous roots (Fig. 2.5b). Does your collection include any other grasses? What kind of roots do they have?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Is there any relation between the type of leaf venation and the type of root of the same plant? How do we find this out?
texts.text: Is there any relation between the type of leaf venation and the type of root of the same plant? How do we find this out?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 2.7: Let us relate and analyse
texts.text: Activity 2.7: Let us relate and analyse

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Collect saplings of five common plants from your school nursery or any other nurseries to plant in your school garden. Examples of such plants can include lemongrass, marigold, sadabahar (periwinkle), and others.
texts.text: ¢ Collect saplings of five common plants from your school nursery or any other nurseries to plant in your school garden. Examples of such plants can include lemongrass, marigold, sadabahar (periwinkle), and others.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Before planting them, observe their roots and the venation in their leaves.
texts.text: ¢ Before planting them, observe their roots and the venation in their leaves.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Record your observations in Table 2.4.
texts.text: ¢ Record your observations in Table 2.4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you observe any relation between the leaf venation and types of root in these plants? A sadabahar plant has a taproot and its leaves have reticulate venation. Do other plants with reticulate venation have taproots too? Lemongrass, on the other hand, has fibrous roots and its leaves have parallel venation. Do other plants with parallel venation have fibrous roots too? Generally, plants with reticulate venation have taproots while those with parallel venation have fibrous roots.
texts.text: Do you observe any relation between the leaf venation and types of root in these plants? A sadabahar plant has a taproot and its leaves have reticulate venation. Do other plants with reticulate venation have taproots too? Lemongrass, on the other hand, has fibrous roots and its leaves have parallel venation. Do other plants with parallel venation have fibrous roots too? Generally, plants with reticulate venation have taproots while those with parallel venation have fibrous roots.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chickpea (chana) is another example of a plant with taproots and reticulate venation in leaves. Wheat is an example of a plant with fibrous roots and parallel venation in its leaves.
texts.text: Chickpea (chana) is another example of a plant with taproots and reticulate venation in leaves. Wheat is an example of a plant with fibrous roots and parallel venation in its leaves.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Is there any relation among the seed of a plant, types of root and leaf venation? Are all seeds similar?

texts.label: caption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You would notice that chickpea seeds are split into two parts (Fig. 2.6a). Each part is called a cotyledon. Plants that have seeds with two cotyledons are called dicotyledons (dicots). Maize has a single thin cotyledon (Fig. 2.6b). Plants with such seeds are called monocotyledons (monocots).
texts.text: You would notice that chickpea seeds are split into two parts (Fig. 2.6a). Each part is called a cotyledon. Plants that have seeds with two cotyledons are called dicotyledons (dicots). Maize has a single thin cotyledon (Fig. 2.6b). Plants with such seeds are called monocotyledons (monocots).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What relation do you observe among leaf venation, root types and the number of cotyledons in seeds of a plant? Dicot plants have reticulate venation and a taproot system while monocot plants have parallel venation and a fibrous root system.
texts.text: What relation do you observe among leaf venation, root types and the number of cotyledons in seeds of a plant? Dicot plants have reticulate venation and a taproot system while monocot plants have parallel venation and a fibrous root system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You have learnt about some features used for grouping plants. Now, let us explore the grouping of animals in more detail.
texts.text: You have learnt about some features used for grouping plants. Now, let us explore the grouping of animals in more detail.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.2 How to group animals?
texts.text: 2.2.2 How to group animals?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Just like plants, animals too are significantly different from one another. How can we group such a wide variety of animals? What features can you think of to group them? In Activity 2.3, you have already set some bases for grouping animals. Let us explore a few of these in more detail.
texts.text: Just like plants, animals too are significantly different from one another. How can we group such a wide variety of animals? What features can you think of to group them? In Activity 2.3, you have already set some bases for grouping animals. Let us explore a few of these in more detail.

texts.orig: Activity 2.9: Let us find out

Which body parts are used by the animals in the poster you created and those in Fig. 2.7 for movement?

texts.text: Table 2.5: Movements in animals and their body parts involved

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What conclusions can you draw from the data given in Table 2.5?
texts.text: What conclusions can you draw from the data given in Table 2.5?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different animals have different types of movement. Animals can fly, run, crawl, walk, hop or jump, and so on. They use different body parts for moving from one place to another. They may use wings, legs, and other parts that help them to move. Here, we have identified animals based on the types of movement and the body parts used for movement. How can we group animals based on their movements? Additionally, many animals differ from each other in shape, size, structure, colour, and other features. Some of these features can also be used to group animals in various ways. Like plants, grouping of animals is important for understanding their diversity.
texts.text: Different animals have different types of movement. Animals can fly, run, crawl, walk, hop or jump, and so on. They use different body parts for moving from one place to another. They may use wings, legs, and other parts that help them to move. Here, we have identified animals based on the types of movement and the body parts used for movement. How can we group animals based on their movements? Additionally, many animals differ from each other in shape, size, structure, colour, and other features. Some of these features can also be used to group animals in various ways. Like plants, grouping of animals is important for understanding their diversity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Janaki Ammal (1897-1984) was an Indian botanist dedicated to environmental work and helped to document and preserve India's rich plant biodiversity. She played a key role in the 'Save Silent Valley' movement. As the head of the Botanical Survey of India, she initiated programmes to document the plant diversity of India.
texts.text: Janaki Ammal (1897-1984) was an Indian botanist dedicated to environmental work and helped to document and preserve India's rich plant biodiversity. She played a key role in the 'Save Silent Valley' movement. As the head of the Botanical Survey of India, she initiated programmes to document the plant diversity of India.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Success Story—Save Silent Valley Movement
texts.text: Success Story—Save Silent Valley Movement

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This is a real story of a forest in the Palakkad district of Kerala. It is about preserving untouched beauty of a moist evergreen forest and its rich biodiversity. The now-famous Silent Valley was saved by a remarkable movement led by common people who were not even residing in the vicinity of the forest. The battle against the proposal of a hydroelectric dam across the Kunthipuzha river persisted for 10 years. At that time, people used all possible available means, such as widespread awareness programmes, letters to editors, articles in, newspapers, seminars, and petitions and appeals in court. The movement was successful in saving the Silent Valley.
texts.text: This is a real story of a forest in the Palakkad district of Kerala. It is about preserving untouched beauty of a moist evergreen forest and its rich biodiversity. The now-famous Silent Valley was saved by a remarkable movement led by common people who were not even residing in the vicinity of the forest. The battle against the proposal of a hydroelectric dam across the Kunthipuzha river persisted for 10 years. At that time, people used all possible available means, such as widespread awareness programmes, letters to editors, articles in, newspapers, seminars, and petitions and appeals in court. The movement was successful in saving the Silent Valley.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3 Plants and Animals in Different Surroundings
texts.text: 2.3 Plants and Animals in Different Surroundings

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might have observed during nature walks that different animals live in different surroundings. You have also recorded movement of animals in Table 2.5. Does the movement of these animals depend upon their surroundings? Let us consider fishes and goat as examples. Fishes live in water. They have streamlined bodies and fins for movement in water (Fig. 2.8a). Goats live in grassy areas and move with the help of legs (Fig. 2.8b). The sizes and shapes of animals also differ from one another.
texts.text: You might have observed during nature walks that different animals live in different surroundings. You have also recorded movement of animals in Table 2.5. Does the movement of these animals depend upon their surroundings? Let us consider fishes and goat as examples. Fishes live in water. They have streamlined bodies and fins for movement in water (Fig. 2.8a). Goats live in grassy areas and move with the help of legs (Fig. 2.8b). The sizes and shapes of animals also differ from one another.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During a discussion in the classroom, Alex recalls that he observed cactus plants with thick and fleshy stems in the deserts of Rajasthan (Fig. 2.9). Maya shares that she saw deodar trees in the Himalayas of Himachal Pradesh (Fig. 2.10). These trees are conical in shape and have flexible and sloping branches.
texts.text: During a discussion in the classroom, Alex recalls that he observed cactus plants with thick and fleshy stems in the deserts of Rajasthan (Fig. 2.9). Maya shares that she saw deodar trees in the Himalayas of Himachal Pradesh (Fig. 2.10). These trees are conical in shape and have flexible and sloping branches.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are your observations regarding plants and animals found in various regions? Discuss your observations with your classmates.
texts.text: What are your observations regarding plants and animals found in various regions? Discuss your observations with your classmates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might observe from Table 2.6 that the plants and animals found in one kind of region are different from those found in
texts.text: You might observe from Table 2.6 that the plants and animals found in one kind of region are different from those found in

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice that these two types of plants found in different regions are different
texts.text: Notice that these two types of plants found in different regions are different

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.10: A deodar tree in the mountains
texts.text: Fig. 2.10: A deodar tree in the mountains

texts.label: text
texts.orig: from each other. Why is it so? Why does the biodiversity of a region vary from that of another? Let us find out.
texts.text: from each other. Why is it so? Why does the biodiversity of a region vary from that of another? Let us find out.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is very little water available in the deserts. A hot desert is typically very hot during the day and very cold at night. Therefore, you will find plants and animals in these areas that can tolerate and survive both the hot conditions during the day and cold conditions at night. The fleshy stems of plants found in the desert can store water and help them tolerate the hot conditions in these places.
texts.text: There is very little water available in the deserts. A hot desert is typically very hot during the day and very cold at night. Therefore, you will find plants and animals in these areas that can tolerate and survive both the hot conditions during the day and cold conditions at night. The fleshy stems of plants found in the desert can store water and help them tolerate the hot conditions in these places.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mountains in extremely cold regions experience frequent snowfall. In order to survive in such conditions, some of the trees have the ability to let the snow slide off easily. Conical shape and sloping branches of deodar trees enable them to do so easily.
texts.text: The mountains in extremely cold regions experience frequent snowfall. In order to survive in such conditions, some of the trees have the ability to let the snow slide off easily. Conical shape and sloping branches of deodar trees enable them to do so easily.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You must have understood by now that the biodiversity varies from region to region because of diverse conditions.
texts.text: You must have understood by now that the biodiversity varies from region to region because of diverse conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Look at the images of a camel from the hot desert of Rajasthan (Fig. 2.11) and a camel from the cold desert of Ladakh (Fig. 2.12). What are the differences you observe between them? What advantages do these differences provide to these camels?
texts.text: Look at the images of a camel from the hot desert of Rajasthan (Fig. 2.11) and a camel from the cold desert of Ladakh (Fig. 2.12). What are the differences you observe between them? What advantages do these differences provide to these camels?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.11: A camel living in the hot desert of Rajasthan
texts.text: Fig. 2.11: A camel living in the hot desert of Rajasthan

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.12: A camel living in the cold desert of Ladakh
texts.text: Fig. 2.12: A camel living in the cold desert of Ladakh

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The camel of the hot desert has long legs with wide hooves. Alex shares that his grandmother told him that the long legs and wide hooves help these camels to walk on the sandy desert without sinking into the sand. On the other hand, the height and legs of camels of a cold desert are comparatively shorter than those found in a hot desert. These short legs allow them to walk easily in mountainous regions.
texts.text: The camel of the hot desert has long legs with wide hooves. Alex shares that his grandmother told him that the long legs and wide hooves help these camels to walk on the sandy desert without sinking into the sand. On the other hand, the height and legs of camels of a cold desert are comparatively shorter than those found in a hot desert. These short legs allow them to walk easily in mountainous regions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In deserts, food is not available easily. Camels store food in their humps. Camels found in the hot desert have one hump each that helps them to survive during the scarcity of food. Camels found in the cold desert have two humps each. These two humps shrink in late winters because there is not much food available in the cold desert and they have to use food stored in their humps during that time. Moreover, they grow long hair from head to neck, which help them survive the cold winters of Ladakh.
texts.text: In deserts, food is not available easily. Camels store food in their humps. Camels found in the hot desert have one hump each that helps them to survive during the scarcity of food. Camels found in the cold desert have two humps each. These two humps shrink in late winters because there is not much food available in the cold desert and they have to use food stored in their humps during that time. Moreover, they grow long hair from head to neck, which help them survive the cold winters of Ladakh.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What other features can help camels to survive in the desert?
texts.text: What other features can help camels to survive in the desert?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Other students also start sharing their observations. Kashi from Rajasthan says that camels excrete small amounts of urine, their dung is dry, and they do not sweat. As camels do not lose much water from their bodies, they can survive for many days without drinking water.
texts.text: Other students also start sharing their observations. Kashi from Rajasthan says that camels excrete small amounts of urine, their dung is dry, and they do not sweat. As camels do not lose much water from their bodies, they can survive for many days without drinking water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maya talks about seeing plants with beautiful bright flowers, rhododendrons, in the Shola forests of Nilgiris. Here, rhododendrons are of shorter height and have smaller leaves to survive through the heavy winds on mountain tops. However, Pema, who is from Sikkim, mentions that she has observed rhododendrons in the nearby mountains to be taller (Fig. 2.13). So, even plants such as rhododendrons may exhibit different features in different regions to survive the conditions of those regions.
texts.text: Maya talks about seeing plants with beautiful bright flowers, rhododendrons, in the Shola forests of Nilgiris. Here, rhododendrons are of shorter height and have smaller leaves to survive through the heavy winds on mountain tops. However, Pema, who is from Sikkim, mentions that she has observed rhododendrons in the nearby mountains to be taller (Fig. 2.13). So, even plants such as rhododendrons may exhibit different features in different regions to survive the conditions of those regions.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2.13: Different features of rhododendrons
texts.text: Fig. 2.13: Different features of rhododendrons

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sagar tells his classmates that he went to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands with his parents for a special event. He saw huge whales and colourful fish in the ocean. His father explained that the streamlined body of fish helps them to swim in water.
texts.text: Sagar tells his classmates that he went to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands with his parents for a special event. He saw huge whales and colourful fish in the ocean. His father explained that the streamlined body of fish helps them to swim in water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have learnt that the plants and animals living in a particular region have special features that make them fit to survive there. The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.
texts.text: We have learnt that the plants and animals living in a particular region have special features that make them fit to survive there. The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The shape of the deodar tree and the height of the rhododendron are adaptations that enable them to survive in the mountainous regions.
texts.text: The shape of the deodar tree and the height of the rhododendron are adaptations that enable them to survive in the mountainous regions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The place where plants and animals live is called their habitat. For example, the habitat of sea turtles is the sea or the ocean. The habitat of a camel is the hot or the cold desert, and the habitat of a rhododendron is the mountains. The habitat of plants and animals provides them food, water, air, shelter and other needs for their survival. Many types of plants and animals may share the same habitat. Habitat plays an important role in shaping the biodiversity of a region.
texts.text: The place where plants and animals live is called their habitat. For example, the habitat of sea turtles is the sea or the ocean. The habitat of a camel is the hot or the cold desert, and the habitat of a rhododendron is the mountains. The habitat of plants and animals provides them food, water, air, shelter and other needs for their survival. Many types of plants and animals may share the same habitat. Habitat plays an important role in shaping the biodiversity of a region.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Know ascientist
texts.text: Know ascientist

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salim Ali (1896-1987) travelled across India to observe diversity in birds. He prepared a list of birds and documented their travel routes and habitats. He recorded the regions with high diversity of birds and took measures to conserve these regions. Keoladeo National Park in Bharatpur, Rajasthan and Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary in Mandya, Karnataka are examples of regions he preserved. He wrote a landmark series of 10 books on birds of the Indian Subcontinent. He is referred to as the 'Birdman of India'. He was awarded Padma Vibhushan in 1976.
texts.text: Salim Ali (1896-1987) travelled across India to observe diversity in birds. He prepared a list of birds and documented their travel routes and habitats. He recorded the regions with high diversity of birds and took measures to conserve these regions. Keoladeo National Park in Bharatpur, Rajasthan and Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary in Mandya, Karnataka are examples of regions he preserved. He wrote a landmark series of 10 books on birds of the Indian Subcontinent. He is referred to as the 'Birdman of India'. He was awarded Padma Vibhushan in 1976.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the different ways in which you can group plants and animals based on their habitats? One way is to group them into those 'that live on land' and those 'that live in water'.
texts.text: What are the different ways in which you can group plants and animals based on their habitats? One way is to group them into those 'that live on land' and those 'that live in water'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The plants and animals that live on land are said to live in terrestrial habitats. Some examples of terrestrial habitats are forests, deserts, grasslands, and mountains.
texts.text: The plants and animals that live on land are said to live in terrestrial habitats. Some examples of terrestrial habitats are forests, deserts, grasslands, and mountains.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The plants and animals that live in water are said to live in aquatic habitats. Some examples of aquatic habitats are ponds, lakes, rivers, and oceans.
texts.text: The plants and animals that live in water are said to live in aquatic habitats. Some examples of aquatic habitats are ponds, lakes, rivers, and oceans.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some animals, such as frogs, can live in water as well as on land. These are called amphibians.
texts.text: Some animals, such as frogs, can live in water as well as on land. These are called amphibians.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What would happen if the habitat of a plant or an animal is damaged? What would happen if a goat does not get grass to eat? Can a fish survive without water?
texts.text: What would happen if the habitat of a plant or an animal is damaged? What would happen if a goat does not get grass to eat? Can a fish survive without water?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Check with your parents, grandparents, and neighbours to know about the plants, birds, insects or any other animal they used to see frequently in their childhood but do not see as often now. These changes often happen when habitats are damaged. The damage to habitats of plants and animals results in loss of their homes, food, and other resources. This leads to the loss of biodiversity.
texts.text: Check with your parents, grandparents, and neighbours to know about the plants, birds, insects or any other animal they used to see frequently in their childhood but do not see as often now. These changes often happen when habitats are damaged. The damage to habitats of plants and animals results in loss of their homes, food, and other resources. This leads to the loss of biodiversity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The population of the Bengal Tiger, Cheetah, and Great Indian Bustard has declined in India due to loss of natural habitats caused by human activities. The Government of India has initiated several projects to conserve our biodiversity. 'Project Tiger' was initiated in 1973 to protect the declining population of the Bengal Tiger. The 'Cheetah Reintroduction Project' was initiated in 2022 to restore the population of the Cheetah. Similarly, habitats of the Great Indian Bustards have been declared as Protected areas in the states of Gujarat, Rajasthan and Maharashtra.
texts.text: The population of the Bengal Tiger, Cheetah, and Great Indian Bustard has declined in India due to loss of natural habitats caused by human activities. The Government of India has initiated several projects to conserve our biodiversity. 'Project Tiger' was initiated in 1973 to protect the declining population of the Bengal Tiger. The 'Cheetah Reintroduction Project' was initiated in 2022 to restore the population of the Cheetah. Similarly, habitats of the Great Indian Bustards have been declared as Protected areas in the states of Gujarat, Rajasthan and Maharashtra.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Cheetah
texts.text: Cheetah

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Great Indian Bustard
texts.text: Great Indian Bustard

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Traditionally Protected Forests: Sacred Groves
texts.text: Traditionally Protected Forests: Sacred Groves

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sacred groves are undisturbed patches of forests. Their sizes may vary from quite small to very large. Sacred groves are found all over India. They are home to different kinds of
texts.text: Sacred groves are undisturbed patches of forests. Their sizes may vary from quite small to very large. Sacred groves are found all over India. They are home to different kinds of

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Sacred grove from the Western Ghats
texts.text: Sacred grove from the Western Ghats

texts.label: text
texts.orig: plants and animals, including numerous medicinal plants. These are protected by the local community and no one is allowed to harm any animals and cut trees in these groves, or disturb the area. This way, sacred groves are a community protected treasure of biodiversity. Find out about the sacred More groves in your region. know!
texts.text: plants and animals, including numerous medicinal plants. These are protected by the local community and no one is allowed to harm any animals and cut trees in these groves, or disturb the area. This way, sacred groves are a community protected treasure of biodiversity. Find out about the sacred More groves in your region. know!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We must protect biodiversity to ensure our planet is full of life, helping plants and animals to survive and thrive.
texts.text: We must protect biodiversity to ensure our planet is full of life, helping plants and animals to survive and thrive.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: We are surrounded by a large variety of plants and animals. Such variety of plants and animals is a part of biodiversity.
texts.text: We are surrounded by a large variety of plants and animals. Such variety of plants and animals is a part of biodiversity.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Plants and animals can be grouped on the basis of similarities and differences among them.
texts.text: Plants and animals can be grouped on the basis of similarities and differences among them.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Plants have similarities and differences based on features associated with roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and so on.
texts.text: Plants have similarities and differences based on features associated with roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and so on.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The method of arranging things into groups based on their common features is called grouping.
texts.text: The method of arranging things into groups based on their common features is called grouping.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Plants can be grouped into herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their heights, types of stem, and branching patterns.
texts.text: Plants can be grouped into herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their heights, types of stem, and branching patterns.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Plants can also be grouped as dicotyledons (dicots) and monocotyledons (monocots) based on the number of cotyledons in their seeds.
texts.text: Plants can also be grouped as dicotyledons (dicots) and monocotyledons (monocots) based on the number of cotyledons in their seeds.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Monocots generally exhibit parallel venation in their leaves and possess fibrous roots while dicots typically exhibit reticulate venation in their leaves and possess taproots.
texts.text: Monocots generally exhibit parallel venation in their leaves and possess fibrous roots while dicots typically exhibit reticulate venation in their leaves and possess taproots.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Animals have different types of movement that can be a basis for their grouping.
texts.text: Animals have different types of movement that can be a basis for their grouping.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Biodiversity of different regions varies because of distinct environmental conditions.
texts.text: Biodiversity of different regions varies because of distinct environmental conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.
texts.text: The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The place where plants and animals live is their habitat.
texts.text: The place where plants and animals live is their habitat.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Based on their habitats, animals and plants can be grouped as terrestrial and aquatic.
texts.text: Based on their habitats, animals and plants can be grouped as terrestrial and aquatic.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Due to damage of their habitats, plants and animals lose their homes, food and other resources resulting in the loss of biodiversity.
texts.text: Due to damage of their habitats, plants and animals lose their homes, food and other resources resulting in the loss of biodiversity.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: We must protect biodiversity to ensure that our planet is full of life, helping plants and animals to survive and thrive.
texts.text: We must protect biodiversity to ensure that our planet is full of life, helping plants and animals to survive and thrive.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Names of some animals are given below. Group them based on their habitats. Write the names of aquatic animals in the area marked 'A' and terrestrial animals in the area marked 'B'. Enter the names of animals living in both habitats in part 'C'.
texts.text: Names of some animals are given below. Group them based on their habitats. Write the names of aquatic animals in the area marked 'A' and terrestrial animals in the area marked 'B'. Enter the names of animals living in both habitats in part 'C'.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Manu's mother maintains a kitchen garden. One day, she was digging out radish from the soil. She told Manu that radish is a kind of root. Examine a radish and write what type of root it is. What type of venation would you observe in the leaves of radish plant?
texts.text: Manu's mother maintains a kitchen garden. One day, she was digging out radish from the soil. She told Manu that radish is a kind of root. Examine a radish and write what type of root it is. What type of venation would you observe in the leaves of radish plant?

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Group the following animals into two groups based on any feature other than those discussed in the chapter— cow, cockroach, pigeon, bat, tortoise, whale, fish, grasshopper, lizard.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. As the population grows and people want more comfortable lives, forests are being cut down to meet various needs. How can this affect our surroundings? How do you think we can address this challenge?
texts.text: As the population grows and people want more comfortable lives, forests are being cut down to meet various needs. How can this affect our surroundings? How do you think we can address this challenge?

texts.orig: 8. Raj argues with his friend Sanjay that "Gudhal (hibiscus) plant is a shrub." What questions can Sanjay ask for clarification?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 10. Observe the labelled part of a duck in the picture given below. What differences do you observe in the feet of the duck compared to the other birds? Which activity would the duck be able to perform using this part?
texts.text: Observe the labelled part of a duck in the picture given below. What differences do you observe in the feet of the duck compared to the other birds? Which activity would the duck be able to perform using this part?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Read about one Indian scientist or a wildlife biologist who is working towards protection of India's biodiversity. Prepare a brief report.
texts.text: ¢ Read about one Indian scientist or a wildlife biologist who is working towards protection of India's biodiversity. Prepare a brief report.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Explore the contributions of Divya Mudappa, Usha Lachungpa, Ghazala Shahabuddin, Nandini Velho, Vidya Athreya, Uma Ramakrishnan and Divya Karnad towards biodiversity in India. Prepare a report of the work done by any three of them.
texts.text: Explore the contributions of Divya Mudappa, Usha Lachungpa, Ghazala Shahabuddin, Nandini Velho, Vidya Athreya, Uma Ramakrishnan and Divya Karnad towards biodiversity in India. Prepare a report of the work done by any three of them.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Label the plants in your school with their local names with the help of your teacher or the gardener. List them in your notebook.
texts.text: Label the plants in your school with their local names with the help of your teacher or the gardener. List them in your notebook.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: With the help of your teacher, plan a field visit or a nature walk. Record your observations. Prepare a class biodiversity register by consolidating the observations and notes of all the students taken during the field visit or nature walk.
texts.text: With the help of your teacher, plan a field visit or a nature walk. Record your observations. Prepare a class biodiversity register by consolidating the observations and notes of all the students taken during the field visit or nature walk.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Find out about 'Project Tiger' and other similar projects initiated in India to protect our biodiversity. Prepare a presentation for your class.
texts.text: Find out about 'Project Tiger' and other similar projects initiated in India to protect our biodiversity. Prepare a presentation for your class.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Divide your class into groups of six students each. Initiate a discussion in the class on how you can protect biodiversity around you. Prepare a group-wise report that includes suggestions given by members of each group.
texts.text: Divide your class into groups of six students each. Initiate a discussion in the class on how you can protect biodiversity around you. Prepare a group-wise report that includes suggestions given by members of each group.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Interact with elders in your family or neighbourhood to find out various plants and animals that they see now but were not seen earlier and vice-versa. Collect pictures of these plants and animals and paste them in a scrapbook. Find out more about them from your teacher.
texts.text: Interact with elders in your family or neighbourhood to find out various plants and animals that they see now but were not seen earlier and vice-versa. Collect pictures of these plants and animals and paste them in a scrapbook. Find out more about them from your teacher.

He is also brilliant at identifying a variety of plants and animals.
